message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Point didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the keeper of the lighthouse, the silence was a companion as constant as the rhythmic *thrum-click* of the great Fresnel lens above. He had spent thirty years watching the sea chew at the cliffs, his life reduced to the maintenance of glass, oil, and solitude.

It was a Tuesday, the kind of gray, heavy day where the horizon seemed to press against the windows, when he found it.

He was walking the perimeter of the rocky shore, checking for debris that might damage the intake valves of the generator. Half-buried in the wet sand, glinting with an unnatural amber hue, was a bottle. It wasn’t the usual green glass of discarded soda or beer. It was thick, hand-blown, sealed with wax that smelled faintly of lavender and old paper.

Elias picked it up. It was heavy. He shook it; there was no sloshing liquid, only the dry rattle of paper. With a pocketknife, he sliced the wax seal. Inside was a single sheet of vellum, rolled tight.

He retreated to the lantern room, the wind howling outside, and unrolled the letter. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably familiar. It was his own.

*“Dear Elias,”* it began.

His heart hammered against his ribs. He looked around the empty room, expecting a prank, a ghost, or a breakdown of his own sanity. He read on.

*“If you are reading this, you have finally stopped looking out and started looking in. You think you are guarding the coast from the sea, but you are guarding yourself from the world. The light doesn’t just warn ships away from the rocks; it calls them home. You have spent three decades afraid
